name: Adorno Noveau
classification_notes: Adorno Noveau blends a humanist sans skeleton with glyphic, Uncial and Gaelic letterform references and Copperplate details, placing it firmly in the experimental display category rather than any conventional text classification.
summary: An experimental display face that splices a humanist sans skeleton with Uncial, Gaelic and Copperplate influences, available as a free personal-use download.
description: Adorno Noveau draws on glyphic typefaces as a starting point before steering towards Uncial and Gaelic letterforms, layering in Copperplate details over a humanist sans-serif structure. The family ships in two styles: a standard Regular and a Borders variant, both in a single weight. Available under a personal-use licence covering one desktop and limited social media reach.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Adorno Noveau as an experimental proposal born from a desire to explore new forms in digital type design. They cite glyphic typefaces as an initial reference, with the design then turning towards Uncial and Gaelic traditions, ultimately mixing a humanist sans-serif skeleton with 15th-century type features and a Copperplate touch. It is offered as a free download for personal use.

name: Armin Grotesk
classification_notes: Designed as an homage to Swiss Style neo-grotesque typography, Armin Grotesk follows the clean, neutral conventions of the neo-grotesque tradition with stylistic alternates that allow for more eclectic expression.
summary: A 14-style neo-grotesque by David Súid, designed as a tribute to Swiss graphic designer Armin Hofmann with stylistic alternates for added flexibility.
description: Designed in 2018 as a homage to Armin Hofmann and the Swiss Style, this neo-grotesque runs across seven weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic. Stylistic sets applied to a broad set of letters including G, R, a, g, h, l, m, n, r, t, u and y allow the typeface to shift character and adapt to different visual languages. The family also includes Armin Soft, a companion with rounded terminals.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Armin Grotesk as an homage to Armin Hofmann and the Swiss Style, noting grids and grotesque typefaces as fundamental tools of that aesthetic. Designer David Súid added stylistic sets across multiple letterforms to make the typeface more eclectic and suitable for diverse visual languages.

name: Armin Soft
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a grotesque and neo-grotesque, designed as a homage to Armin Hofmann's visual language; the 'Soft' variant implies softened terminals or curves relative to the parent Armin Grotesk.
summary: A softened grotesque in 14 styles, designed as an homage to Armin Hofmann's Swiss visual language with stylistic alternates for added flexibility.
description: Armin Soft is the rounded counterpart to Armin Grotesk, both conceived as tributes to the graphic vocabulary associated with Armin Hofmann. Seven weights from Thin to Black each come with an italic, and stylistic sets offer alternate forms for letters including G, R, a, g and t, giving designers room to vary the texture of a layout. The result is a grotesk that sits between Swiss neutrality and softer contemporary sensibilities.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry present Armin Soft as the softest variant of Armin Grotesk, a family designed by David Súid as a homage to the work of Armin Hofmann. Stylistic sets applied to key letterforms make it more eclectic and adaptable for varied visual languages, with 14 styles available across seven weights with matching italics.

name: ARP
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a neo-grotesk type system; combines functional grotesque structure with contrasted weights and glyphic display alternates, including an inverted-contrast light cut.
summary: A neo-grotesque type system by Felipe Sanzana spanning five weights, with an inverted-contrast light, a sharp mid-weight, and a high-contrast heavy, plus a Display subfamily with geometric and glyphic alternates.
description: The family runs across ten styles in two subfamilies — ARP and ARP Display — with each offering five weights numbered 20 through 240. Short ascenders and descenders improve legibility at smaller sizes, ink traps extend usability into display contexts, and an extensive set of icons, arrows and symbols supports consistent layouts across digital and print. All 640 characters per style include variable font versions of both subfamilies.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe ARP as a neo-grotesk type system exploring the relations between contrast, functionality, and graphic character within one family. It includes five weights from fine inverted-contrast strokes to a high-contrast heavyweight, short ascenders and descenders for legibility, ink traps for display use, and a wide range of icons and symbols. The Display subfamily adds geometric and glyphic style alternates to expand versatility.

name: Baile
classification_notes: Described as a serif display typeface inspired by ITC Benguiat, with decorative terminals and adjusted proportions suited to headline and poster use rather than text setting.
summary: A decorative display serif by Gaspar Muñoz, drawing on ITC Benguiat with reworked proportions and distinctive terminals for headline and poster use.
description: Designed by Gaspar Muñoz, Baile takes ITC Benguiat as its starting point and reworks it with adjusted proportions, revised terminals and an extrabold weight for added visual punch. Available in Regular and Italic, the two-style family suits impactful headlines, book covers, posters and editorial layouts where a decorative serif personality is needed. Its ornamental character keeps it firmly in display territory rather than running text.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Baile as a serif display typeface inspired by ITC Benguiat, redesigned with adjusted proportions, distinctive terminals and an extrabold style for greater visual weight. They position it for impactful headlines, book covers, posters and editorial layouts, and offer it in Regular and Italic styles for versatility across creative applications.

name: Arrieta
classification_notes: Arrieta draws from Alexander Phemister's Old Style series tradition but reduces curves, blending punch-cutting mechanics with nib-pen strokes into a form that sits between old-style and transitional serif territory.
summary: A serif family by Diego Aravena that reduces curves relative to Phemister's Old Style tradition, producing an angular, mechanically inflected text face across four weights.
description: Arrieta blends the mechanical discipline of punch-cutting with the stroke quality of a nib pen, arriving at a typeface that preserves the texture of Old Style serifs while deliberately avoiding curves. The family runs across eight styles: Regular, Medium, Bold and Black, each with a matching italic. Its angled character gives it a distinctive rhythm suited to editorial and identity contexts where a less conventional serif is wanted.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Arrieta as a versatile family combining the traditional punch-cutting process with expressive nib-pen strokes, producing a reliable, angled typeface. They frame it through a myth about a punchcutter who avoided curves out of laziness, with the goal of preserving the texture of Alexander Phemister's Old Styles series while incorporating fewer curves into the design. Designed by Diego Aravena and released in 2023, it comes in eight styles.

name: Becky
classification_notes: Becky is explicitly described as a display type inspired by geometric classics such as Futura, with informal and edgy details. Its primary use cases are headlines, branding and advertising, placing it firmly in the display category with a geometric sans influence.
summary: A geometric display family of 10 styles by Bana Arasanz, aimed at advertising, branding and retail work targeting a young creative audience.
description: Inspired by Futura and similar geometric classics, Becky introduces an informal, slightly edgy quality through subtly curved verticals. Five weights run from Light to Extrabold, each with a matching italic, giving 10 styles in total across 445 characters. Two stylistic sets extend its range: SS1 straightens curved diagonals for a more formal appearance at smaller sizes, while SS2 offers alternate glyphs for a handful of characters.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Becky as a versatile display type designed for a young, creative and up-to-date audience, geared towards advertising and retail. They cite Futura and similar geometric classics as inspiration, noting the typeface's edgy, informal character in details such as the subtle curve of its verticals. The family comprises 10 styles across 5 weights with two stylistic sets offering more formal or alternate glyph options.

name: Campora
classification_notes: A geometric display family derived from a mashup of Dynamo and Avant Garde, combining sharp geometric construction with alternate ligatures; its decorative Inline variant reinforces its display-first intent.
summary: A geometric display family by Salvador Rodriguez, blending the sharp angles of Dynamo with the landmark ligature system of Avant Garde Gothic.
description: Inspired by a hand-painted store sign spotted in Bologna, this family combines the angular sharpness of Dynamo with the geometric structure and alternate ligatures associated with Avant Garde Gothic. The family spans 19 styles across three subfamilies: Campora, Campora Classic and a single decorative Campora Inline, each sharing a nine-weight range from Thin to Heavy. The OpenType ligature features are a central part of the design's character, making it well suited to headlines, logotypes and display work.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Campora as inspired by a store sign they photographed in Bologna's Piazza Maggiore during the Bologna Children's Book Fair. On returning to the studio they identified the lettering as Dynamo, and proceeded to design a new typeface that fuses Dynamo's sharp geometric design with the revolutionary ligatures of Avant Garde Gothic. The result spans 19 styles across three subfamilies, with new weights and OpenType features.

name: Canilari
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a post-modern type family; the angular shapes and high-contrast details place it in a display-oriented contemporary serif category inspired by indigenous decorative motifs.
summary: A post-modern serif by Patricio Truenos drawing on the angular decorative patterns of Diaguita pottery from pre-colonial northern Chile and Argentina.
description: Designed by Patricio Truenos and awarded at Bienal Tipos Latinos 2014, Canilari translates the geometric decorative language of Diaguita ceramics into a high-personality serif family. Four weights with italics plus an ornaments set are offered in two character sets: Std (369 characters) and Pro (710 characters), together covering 219 languages. The angular shapes and consistent texture make it workable across continuous text in publishing contexts and high-impact headline or poster use.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Canilari as a post-modern type family inspired by Diaguita pottery, a cultural tradition from 10th–16th century A.D. that settled in what is now northern Chile and northwest Argentina. They position it as a statement of identity that brings native American visual culture to a global stage, suited to newspapers, magazines, books, posters, logotypes and advertising. The family includes ornaments and illuminated caps, and was selected at the Bienal Tipos Latinos 2014.

name: Cassius
classification_notes: The foundry places Cassius within the Garaldes family, a branch of old-style serifs rooted in Renaissance letterforms. Its inverted anatomy and projected terminals give it a postmodern inflection while retaining the broad structural logic of a garalde.
summary: A postmodern garalde with inverted anatomy and projected terminals, offered in five weights with matching italics and a variable font axis.
description: Designed by David Súid, Cassius takes the structural logic of a Garaldes serif and reworks it through inverted anatomy and projected terminals to produce something with a distinctive rhythmic character. The family runs from Regular to Black across ten upright and italic styles, with a variable font available for each axis. OpenType features include small caps, old-style figures, ligatures, fractions and case-sensitive punctuation, making it well suited to book and magazine composition.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describes Cassius and Cassius Italic as a postmodern typeface system from the Garaldes family, distinguished by its inverted anatomy and projected terminals. Designed with close attention to structure, the family aims to bring personality and rhythm to printed and digital text compositions through five weights, matching italics and a full variable font offering.

name: Cannon
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as combining neo-grotesque clarity with geometric touches, placing it firmly in the neo-grotesque subcategory with secondary geometric influence.
summary: A neo-grotesque with geometric inflections across nine weights and matching italics, aimed at advertising, brand identity and packaging work.
description: Designed by Patricio Truenos, Cannon blends the clean structure of a neo-grotesque with geometric details to produce a typeface the foundry describes as carrying retro and technological character. The family runs to 18 styles across nine weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, and includes small caps and alternate characters throughout. It suits bold headlines, short texts and high-impact brand applications where a familiar grotesque skeleton needs a little more visual energy.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Cannon as the result of combining clean neo-grotesque aesthetics with a touch of geometric design, producing a simple but dynamic typeface with retro and technological airs. The family comprises 36 variants and 9 weights with matching italics, small caps, alternate characters and extended language support, making it suitable for advertising, brand identities, packaging and high-impact headlines.

name: Cenzo Flare
classification_notes: A modern sans serif with flared terminals drawn from Americana advertising and vintage poster lettering, placing it firmly in the display category with a retro-inflected flared sans subcategory.
summary: A 40-style display family by Bana Arasanz drawing on vintage Americana advertising and Italian poster lettering, with flared terminals and four decorative subfamilies designed for layering.
description: Built on a modern sans base with subtly flared terminals, the family takes its cues from old American advertising, Italian posters and mid-century signage. Four subfamilies — Regular, Inline, Line and Condensed — each run across five weights with matching italics, allowing the decorative variants to be stacked as colour layers for poster and branding work. OpenType alternates and extended language support broaden its range beyond headline use.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Cenzo Flare as a blend of modern sans serif and flared detail, inspired by old Americana advertising, Italian posters, century-old logos and signage, combined with the current trend for retro typography in TV series and music imagery. Designed for headlines, posters, advertising and corporate identity, the 40-font family spans four subfamilies with multiple weights, italics, OpenType alternates and extended language support.

name: Eclipsa
designer: Joaquín Contreras Soto, Diego Aravena Silo
classification_notes: Inspired by type used in an old workers' newspaper and formally associated with Art Deco, it features rounded corners and partially filled counters, placing it firmly in the display category with an art deco subcategory.
summary: A display typeface drawn from the type of an old workers' newspaper, with rounded corners and partially closed counters that give it a tactile, softened quality.
description: Eclipsa takes its formal cues from Art Deco, referencing the movement's poster and architectural lettering traditions. The rounded corners and filled-in internal spaces of certain letters lend a physical, almost stamped character that distinguishes it from more clinical revivals of the period. It covers Latin and Cyrillic scripts across 229 languages in a single Regular style of 323 glyphs.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Eclipsa as a headline typeface inspired by type found in an old workers' newspaper. The absence of internal counters in some letters and the rounded corners give it a physical, soft character. They associate it historically with the formal style of the Art Deco movement, known for its posters and architectural design. Latin and Cyrillic alphabets are included, covering 229 languages, with the Cyrillic drawn by Contreras and Diego Aravena Silo.

name: Collab Type
classification_notes: A project-based display typeface assembled from hundreds of individual letter contributions across nine workshops; its expressive, non-uniform character places it firmly in the display category rather than any conventional text classification.
summary: An ongoing collaborative typeface project by W Type Foundry, assembled from workshops held across nine cities with over 400 contributors from 42 countries.
description: Started in 2021, the Collabtype project runs workshops in universities and events worldwide, inviting non-specialists to design individual letterforms using both digital and analogue methods. Each session produces a distinct typeface edition, digitalised by the W Type Foundry team, with nine versions released to date covering New York, Barcelona, Santiago, Lisbon and beyond. All editions are available for free download under a personal use licence.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Collabtype as their initiative to design the world's largest typeface in collaboration with diverse contributors who need no specialist design background. Workshops run both remotely and in person using digital and analogue methods, concluding with the foundry's team digitalising the results. The project has so far produced nine typefaces from over 1,300 letters designed by an estimated 427 authors across 42 nationalities, and all are available for free personal use download.

name: Fansan
classification_notes: Fansan draws directly on Art Nouveau typographic tradition, sharing lineage with Benguiat, Windsor and Melbourne. Its top-heavy stress, sharp terminals and organic curves place it firmly in the decorative serif category with a strong display orientation.
summary: An Art Nouveau serif by Diego Aravena, taking cues from Benguiat, Windsor and Melbourne across two optical subfamilies and five weights.
description: Top-heavy stress runs consistently through the character set, giving lighter weights an elegant, pointed quality while heavier styles take on a more playful, expressive character. Sharp terminals on the lowercase a, f and g bridge the upper and lowercase forms, lending the family visual coherence across its range. With ten styles split between a text and a display subfamily, Fansan is built primarily for headline and display settings where typographic personality is the brief.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Fansan as an Art Nouveau type family with roman, italic and optical sizes, rooted in references such as Benguiat, Windsor and Melbourne. They highlight the top-heavy stress applied consistently across the character set as a central design challenge, and note the sharp terminals of characters like the a, f and g as a deliberate link between upper and lowercase forms. The family is positioned for display use where personality is required.

name: Friends
classification_notes: The specimens and described use cases suggest a clean, contemporary sans-serif with geometric sensibility, designed for postmodern graphic applications including web and editorial work.
summary: A 14-style contemporary sans with matching italics, icons and emoji glyphs, pitched at postmodern graphic and editorial work.
description: Designed by David Súid, Friends spans seven weights from Thin to Black with italic counterparts throughout, plus a suite of icons, emojis, arrows and ligatures. The family positions itself as a visual language system, drawing on emoji and contemporary graphic culture as formal influences. Suited to web design, posters, branding and editorial contexts where a clean, modern sans with expressive supplementary characters is needed.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Friends as a complete and contemporary language system for postmodern graphics, inspired by new forms of visual communication including emojis and contemporary graphic arts. The family includes 14 styles plus italics, and is recommended for web design, book design, posters, editorial design, branding, advertising, headlines and short texts.

name: Fuse
classification_notes: Fuse blends geometric structure (DIN, Futura) with humanist rhythm (Meta Pro, The Sans), placing it squarely in the humanist sans tradition despite its rational, condensed qualities.
summary: A humanist sans drawing on the rhythm of Meta Pro and The Sans while borrowing geometric structure from DIN and Futura, available in nine weights with matching italics.
description: Designed by Diego Aravena and Salvador Rodriguez, Fuse sets out to balance humanist warmth with rational, condensed geometry across 18 styles. OpenType features include alternative glyphs, old-style and lining numerals, fractions, superscripts, subscripts, ligatures and small caps. The family is positioned broadly across graphic design, print, web, motion and branding work.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Fuse as a typeface inspired by two typographic traditions, merging the rhythm of humanist faces like Meta Pro and The Sans with the geometric terminations and structures of DIN and Futura. The result is a condensed, parallel-rhythm typeface that is simple and functional, packed with OpenType features and suited to graphic design, printed publications, web design, motion graphics, interaction design and branding.

name: Fujiwara
classification_notes: Fujiwara is explicitly described as a neo-grotesque, drawing on modern Swiss typeface proportions. It splits into two subfamilies: a sharp-contrast cut (A) and a rounded counterform cut (B), both following grotesque conventions.
summary: A neo-grotesque in two subfamilies by David Súid, splitting sharp contrast and rounded counterforms across 20 styles with variable font options.
description: Designed by David Súid, Fujiwara draws on modern Swiss typeface proportions and adds a personal touch through two distinct subfamilies: Fujiwara A, with sharp contrast, and Fujiwara B, with rounded counterforms. Both run from Light to Black with matching italics, and each is available as a variable font. OpenType features include fractions, tabular lining figures, stylistic alternates, ligatures, case-sensitive forms and emojis.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Fujiwara as a neo-grotesque built around sharp contrast and carefully aligned counterforms, offered in two subfamilies: 'A' for sharp contrast and 'B' for display rounded counterforms. The family spans 20 styles across five weights with italics, two variable options, and a broad set of OpenType features. The foundry note that the slanted versions were very carefully drawn and corrected.

name: Gardenia
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a grotesk sans-serif by the foundry, with characteristics typical of the grotesque tradition: strong legibility at text sizes and expressive extremes at display weights.
summary: A nine-weight grotesque sans by Salvador Rodríguez, spanning Thin to Ultrablack with matching italics for both text and display use.
description: Designed by Salvador Rodríguez between 2015 and 2016, this grotesque sans runs across 18 styles covering nine weights from Thin to Ultrablack, each with a matching italic. The mid-range weights are tuned for legibility in body text, while the extreme cuts at Black and Thin read as strong display faces. A broad OpenType feature set rounds out a family aimed at graphic design, editorial and screen applications.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Gardenia as a grotesk sans-serif designed by Salvador Rodríguez in 2015/2016, coming in nine weights with matching italics. They note it is characterised by legibility at medium sizes, with the Black and Thin weights performing particularly well at display sizes, and that it suits longer texts, headlines, graphic design, web, print, motion graphics and interaction design, backed by a wide range of OpenType features.

name: Fuse V.2
classification_notes: Fuse V.2 is a geometric sans-serif with fully rounded corners throughout, giving it a soft, approachable quality across both display and text subfamilies.
summary: A rounded extension of the Fuse family, split into Display and Text subfamilies across ten weights with italics throughout.
description: Every character corner in this family is rounded, producing a softer, more approachable result than the original Fuse. Thirty-six styles span Thin to Ultrablack across two subfamilies optimised for display and text use respectively. OpenType features include alternates, ligatures, small caps, fractions and arrows, making it reasonably well-equipped for demanding graphic design work.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Fuse V.2 as an extension of their popular Fuse family, differentiated by fully rounded corners that make it a friendlier and more amiable variant. Designed by Diego Aravena and Salvador Rodriguez, the 36 fonts form part of the larger Fuse super family and are built with OpenType features including alternates, ligatures, fractions, small caps and extended language support, suited to both display and text applications.

name: Gallos
classification_notes: Gallos is rooted in the Uncial script tradition, blending historical calligraphic letterforms with geometric rationalism and Gaelic influences, placing it firmly in the display category with a specialised uncial subcategory.
summary: An 18-style type family that fuses Uncial calligraphic tradition with geometric rationalism across two distinct subfamilies, one referencing Paul Renner's Architype models.
description: Gallos splits into two subfamilies, Gallos Uncial and Gallos Architype, differentiated by key letterforms including the 'a', 'r', 'm', 'n' and 'w'. The Uncial subfamily retains closed bowls and classical Uncial curves, while the Architype subfamily opens counters and introduces a stem-and-dot 'r' drawn from Renner's rationalist experiments. Both share curved strokes on the 'e' and 't', Gaelic-inflected 'K' and 'X' forms, and curved descenders that tie the system together across nine weights from Thin to Heavy.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Gallos as an impossible-seeming combination of Architype, Geometric, Gaelic and Uncial influences, made real across 18 styles. The two subfamilies share classical Uncial features while diverging in their treatment of specific letterforms, with the Architype model drawing on Paul Renner's rational geometric experiments and the Uncial model leaning into historical calligraphic forms with Gaelic character.

name: Hardbop
classification_notes: A condensed display typeface with grotesque DNA, drawing on hand-painted signage, Impact, and Bebas. Its exaggerated counterforms and extreme weight range put it squarely in the display category rather than a functional grotesque.
summary: A condensed display grotesque by Mauro Andrés, drawn from jazz-era Blue Note record cover design and hand-painted sign lettering.
description: Eight weights from Thin to Black make up this poster-oriented family, with exaggerated counterforms and terminals referencing both classic compressed grotesques like Impact and Bebas and the bold graphic language of Reid Miles's Blue Note sleeve designs. Japanese jazz album covers of the 1970s also inform its character, giving it a particular attitude suited to titles, covers and large-scale applications. Not designed for running text, it is built for contexts where visibility is the primary concern.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Hardbop as a typographic system inspired by jazz, particularly the hard bop style, and by Reid Miles's graphic work for Blue Note Records in the 1950s, as well as Japanese jazz album covers of the 1970s and condensed hand-painted signage. It references classic typefaces including Impact, Bebas, Din, Frontage and TT Trailers, with certain characteristics such as counterforms and endings pushed to deliberate extremes. The family is designed for titles, covers and posters where it is intended not to go unnoticed.

name: Hartwell
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as a Neo-humanist sans, combining calligraphic stroke terminals drawn from humanist tradition with geometric touches, placing it squarely in the humanist sans subcategory.
summary: A neo-humanist sans by David Súid that blends calligraphic stroke terminals with geometric touches across 36 styles in two subfamilies.
description: Hartwell pairs the rhythmic, pen-influenced strokes of humanist sans tradition with geometric details, resulting in what the foundry terms a postmodern sans. Nine weights from Thin to Heavy are offered with matching italics, and a second subfamily, Hartwell Alt, mirrors the full weight range with alternate glyph forms. OpenType features include arrows, ligatures, fractions and alternate glyphs, positioning the family for editorial, branding and advertising work.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describes Hartwell as a Neo-humanist sans serif type family whose strokes and terminals draw from calligraphic humanist shapes combined with geometric touches. The result, they say, is a versatile postmodern family suited to editorial design, branding, advertising, headlines and short texts, available in 36 styles across two subfamilies with broad OpenType feature support.

name: Hattrick
classification_notes: A display superfamily drawing on varsity lettering and retro sports aesthetics, with three subfamilies spanning sans, serif and a semi hybrid with monospaced rhythm. Its expressive width range and athletic references place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A 110-style superfamily by Gaspar Muñoz drawing on varsity lettering and retro American sports graphics, with sans, serif and semi subfamilies across five widths.
description: The family spans Compressed through to Expanded across five weights from ExtraLight to Extrabold, each with italics, giving designers a wide typographic range within a single athletic idiom. The Semi subfamily introduces a near-monospaced rhythm that adds technical tension while the Serif branch brings a more editorial register. Aimed at sports branding, packaging and identity work where consistent visual impact is needed across scales.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Hattrick as a versatile type superfamily inspired by classic varsity lettering and retro sports aesthetics, built to embody the energy of college jerseys, American football uniforms and traditional athletic graphics. With 110 styles across Sans, Serif and a hybrid Semi subfamily, it functions as a complete typographic system for sports branding, editorial design, packaging, merchandising and identity, suited to both high-impact headlines and intricate typographic environments.

name: Hermann
classification_notes: Inspired by Sabon and Garamond, Hermann draws on 20th-century book typography traditions with humanist proportions, swashes and discretionary ligatures that bridge text and display use.
summary: A humanist text serif with swashes and discretionary ligatures, designed around the literary and conceptual worlds of Hermann Hesse and Aldous Huxley.
description: Developed by Diego Aravena and Salvador Rodriguez, Hermann takes Sabon and Garamond as structural references and weaves in concepts of duality and surrealism drawn from 20th-century novel culture. The family spans ten styles across five weights from Regular to Heavy, each with a matching italic, and the character set includes full sets of capital discretionary ligatures, swashes from A to Z, and final letter forms that give the typeface range beyond the text column. Extended German proofing supports the Central European language coverage implied by its literary namesake.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Hermann as one of their most readable typefaces to date, conceived after close study of the literary and philosophical ideas behind The Steppenwolf and Brave New World. The design team drew on the typographic conventions of both Hesse and Huxley's published works, citing Sabon and Garamond as key references, and set out to combine legibility with wildness. Capital discretionary ligatures, swashes from A to Z, and letter final forms across both roman and italic styles were designed to allow the typeface to function as both a text and display face.

name: Helios
classification_notes: Described as a blend of early sans-serif tradition with contemporary sensibility, featuring open terminals, rational structure and slightly wider proportions than classic grotesques. The Stencil subfamily adds a display dimension but the core family sits firmly in the grotesque sans tradition.
summary: A grotesque sans in two subfamilies, Antique and Stencil, spanning nine weights with italics and a wider-than-typical structure for stronger visual impact.
description: Designed by Salvador Rodriguez Felipe Sanzana, Helios takes the rational skeleton of early sans-serifs and widens it slightly beyond historical norms to increase on-page presence. Open terminals and deeper contrast at stroke junctions improve legibility, while distinctive details such as the leg of the R and the tail of the Q give the family a recognisable character. The Stencil subfamily mirrors the full weight range and shifts between neutral and display qualities depending on size.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Helios as a fusion of early sans-serif structure and contemporary design thinking, with a subtly wider-than-average skeleton, open terminals and increased stroke contrast for better balance of positive and negative space. The Stencil subfamily is presented as the tougher counterpart, with rigorously measured gaps that read neutrally at small sizes and shift to a display logic at larger scales. The complete family comprises 36 styles with 782 glyphs, small caps, and extensive OpenType features including alternates, ligatures, fractions and extended language support.

name: Herokid
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a grotesque style font inspired by Helvetica, Impact and Univers, placing it firmly in the grotesque sans-serif tradition with an unusually wide range of width variants.
summary: A grotesque sans spanning eight weights and six widths from UltraCondensed to Expanded, with a full italic counterpart across all 98 styles.
description: Inspired by Helvetica, Impact and Univers, this large grotesque family by Gaspar Muñoz covers an unusually broad width range alongside eight weights, making it practical for work that demands typographic contrast within a single family. Heavier weights feature tight counterforms suited to large display headlines, while the regular and lighter cuts are intended for body copy. A variable font is included alongside the 98 static styles, and a complete family licence is available at USD 189.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Herokid as a grotesque with a dynamic, versatile and flexible personality, ranging from Thin to Heavy and from UltraCompressed to UltraExpanded across 98 styles and 5 subfamilies. They position it as a workhorse capable of generating high-impact headlines, subtitles and body text within a single family, with wide and condensed widths enabling movement and contrast in layouts.

name: Honest
classification_notes: A display serif drawing on 1970s–80s print media letterforms, with a large x-height and distinctive style suited to headlines and logos rather than continuous text.
summary: A display serif by Diego Aravena that revisits 1970s and 1980s print typography across seven weights with upright and italic styles.
description: Honest takes its formal cues from the serif typefaces found in print media during the 1970s and 1980s, giving it a distinctly period-inflected character that works well for logos and headline settings. Its large x-height suits tight leading, which makes it practical for compact headline compositions. The family runs from Thin to Heavy, each with a matching italic, totalling fourteen styles.
foundry_blurb: Honest is described by W Type Foundry as drawing inspiration from serif fonts prevalent in print media during the 1970s and 1980s. Its letter shapes are suited to prominent uses such as logos and striking headlines, and its large x-height makes it appropriate for tight leading. The family offers seven weights in Standard and Italic styles, designed by Diego Aravena.

name: Huemul Slab
classification_notes: Huemul Slab is a low-contrast slab serif that bridges Clarendon's transitional proportions with Rockwell's classic character, placing it between geometric and transitional slab categories but closer to geometric given its aligned heights and even structure.
summary: A slab serif by Diego Aravena that merges Rockwell's classic letterform character with the more even, wider proportions associated with Clarendon and transitional typefaces.
description: Designed as a bridge between two slab serif traditions, Huemul Slab takes the structural personality of Rockwell and applies the more uniform, transitional proportions of Clarendon to produce a low-contrast face with aligned cap and x-heights. Four weights from Regular to Black cover the core range without italics or condensed cuts. The result suits display and editorial settings where a contemporary slab is needed without the rigid geometry of a pure Rockwell revival.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Huemul Slab as a homage to both Clarendon and Rockwell, noting that while each features powerful slab serifs their proportions differ considerably. Clarendon's transitional proportions produce a wider, more even structure, while Rockwell follows classic proportions with greater variation between letterforms. Huemul Slab aims to combine Rockwell's character with transitional proportions for a contemporary result, offering a low-contrast typeface with aligned heights and a touch of classic elegance.

name: Hofmann Grotesk
classification_notes: Explicitly described as grotesque in style, with Swiss typographic influences from Armin Hofmann; features organic, fluid terminals that distinguish it from more geometric or humanist grotesques.
summary: A grotesque family in 22 styles, inspired by Swiss designer Armin Hofmann, with three alternate character sets and a width axis spanning Compressed to Expanded.
description: Designed by David Súid, the family spans two subfamilies: a core grotesque range from Thin to Black and a width-focused extension covering Compressed, Medium and Expanded cuts, each with italics. Three alternate character sets — default, display and classic — allow the personality to shift from neutral text use to more expressive, short-form compositions. The Expanded subfamily takes a more organic direction, with fluid curves and harmonious endings suited to larger sizes.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Hofmann Grotesk as a grotesque inspired by Swiss designer Armin Hofmann, built across 22 styles and two subfamilies. The core family offers three alternate sets — default for versatility, display for striking compositions, and classic for distraction-free text — while the Expanded subfamily leans into more organic, fluid forms with its own pair of alternate sets emphasising either natural figures or humanistic blends.

name: Human Max
classification_notes: Explicitly presented as a Display option, Human Max is a geometric sans revival derived from Max Bill's experimental typeface study, with contemporary geometric refinements and alternate characters distinguishing it from standard text use.
summary: A display revival of Max Bill's experimental 'study for a typeface for machines and humans', adapted by David Súid with a contemporary geometric finish across seven weights.
description: Based on Max Bill's original typographic study, the design has been reworked into a functional display system with stylistic alternates, ligatures and extended punctuation to aid readability in complex settings. It shares proportional DNA with W Type Foundry's Hofmann Grotesk, positioning the two as complementary faces within a broader geometric tradition. Seven weights from Thin to Black make it suited to editorial and identity work where a Bauhaus-adjacent geometric voice is required.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Human Max as a revival of Max Bill's 'study for a typeface for machines and humans', designed by David Súid. They note it complements Hofmann Grotesk through shared proportions and origin, while being adapted with a contemporary geometric finish. The family is presented as a display option offering stylistic alternates, ligatures, punctuation, symbols, numerals and accents across seven styles.

name: Joane
classification_notes: Joane draws explicitly from French didone traditions with high contrast strokes, calligraphic endings and glyphic serifs, placing it squarely in the didone category.
summary: A French didone-inspired serif by Ale Navarro that blends calligraphic endings and glyphic serifs, with engraved and deco variants extending its range.
description: Joane takes cues from French didone traditions, combining high-contrast strokes with calligraphic terminals and glyphic serifs for a style that sits between classical warmth and typographic rigour. The family spans eight weights with matching italics, plus two specialist sub-families: Joane Deco and Joane Engraved, which broaden its application across decorative and print contexts. Suited to magazines, packaging, branding and editorial work where a refined serif with some personality is wanted.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Joane as mixing the elegance of French didones with calligraphic endings and glyphic serifs, resulting in a warm and unique style. Its curves are noted as a particular strength, and the addition of engraved and deco versions adds versatility. The family is positioned for use in magazines, branding, advertising, labels, web and packaging.

name: Joane PRO
classification_notes: Joane Pro is a high-contrast serif with sharp features drawing on French 16th-century, Dutch 17th-century and English 18th-century typographic models, placing it in the tradition of classical transitional and Didone serifs.
summary: A high-contrast serif by Ale Navarro drawing on three centuries of European typographic tradition, offered across five optical sizes and nine weights.
description: Built on the original Joane (2018), this expanded Pro version spans 79 styles across five optical sizes from XS to XL, each tuned for its intended setting from small text to large display work. Nine weights from Thin to Black, each with an italic, give considerable range within each optical subfamily. The sharp features and organic texture reflect sources in French, Dutch and English punchcutting traditions across the 16th to 18th centuries.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Joane Pro as a new typeface built on Joane (2018), rooted in French 16th-century typography, Dutch 17th-century models and English 18th-century type. The result is a high-contrast, sharp-featured typeface with organic texture, available in 79 styles across five subfamilies suited to both display and small-size use.

name: Kappa
classification_notes: Described by the foundry as a modern sans serif with both humanistic and geometric features, placing it in a hybrid humanist-geometric subcategory rather than a pure grotesque or geometric.
summary: A slightly narrow modern sans serif in 36 styles across Display and Text subfamilies, designed with an extended weight range and broad OpenType feature support.
description: Designed by Diego Aravena and Salvador Rodriguez, Kappa runs from Thin to Ultrablack in both Display and Text optical cuts, each with corresponding italics. Its slightly condensed proportions and tall x-height aim to improve legibility at small sizes while fitting more text on the page. OpenType features include small caps, alternates, ligatures, fractions and extended language support across all weights.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Kappa as a modern sans serif combining humanistic and geometric traits, with a slightly narrow structure designed to suit a wide range of platforms and to save paper in print. The taller cap height aids legibility at small sizes. The 36-style super family is split into Display and Text subfamilies and is loaded with OpenType features including alternates, ligatures, fractions, arrows, small caps and extended language support.

name: Juno
classification_notes: Juno is explicitly described as a script font drawn from freehand strokes and Latin American vernacular sign-painting, placing it firmly in the casual or informal script category designed for display use.
summary: A display script drawn from Latin American vernacular sign-painting traditions, available in 40 styles across five width subfamilies.
description: Inspired by the freehand lettering of Latin American street signs, Juno blends loose, brush-like strokes with typographic discipline across a large family of 40 styles. Five width subfamilies run from Condensed to Expanded, each offering four weights from Regular to Black with true italics throughout. OpenType alternates and extended language support broaden its use across packaging, headlines and handmade-feel branding.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Juno as a soft and friendly script font for display use, inspired by Latin American vernacular signs and defined by the freshness of freehand strokes combined with typographic rigour. The family spans 40 styles across five subfamilies — Regular, Semi Condensed, Condensed, Semi Expanded and Expanded — with variables moving between regular and black weights, and condensed to expanded widths, plus true italics and powerful OpenType features.

name: Kappa V.2
classification_notes: A serif companion to the Kappa sans family, sharing its slightly narrowed structure and tall ascenders, designed for both text and display use.
summary: A serif companion to the Kappa sans family, sharing its slightly narrowed proportions and tall ascenders across 36 styles split between text and display subfamilies.
description: Designed by Salvador Rodriguez, Kappa V.2 is the serif counterpart to Kappa sans, built with a subtly condensed structure and prominent ascender height that makes it adaptable across print and screen contexts. The family spans nine weights from Thin to Ultrablack with matching italics, repeated across text and display subfamilies for a total of 36 styles. OpenType features include alternates, ligatures, fractions, small caps and extended language support.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Kappa V.2 as the serif version of their popular Kappa sans, sharing the same slightly narrowed structure and prominent ascender height for broad platform suitability. They note that the serif construction improves legibility accuracy, making it especially effective when paired with Kappa sans. The 36 fonts form part of the larger Kappa super family and are designed with extensive OpenType features for graphic design and display or text applications.

name: Mamba
classification_notes: Inspired by Cooper Black, Mamba belongs to the fat face display tradition characterised by heavy rounded strokes and bold presence suited to headlines and decorative use.
summary: A two-style display face by Gaspar Muñoz drawing directly from the Cooper Black tradition, offered in Regular and Oblique.
description: Mamba takes Cooper Black as its starting point, working within the fat face display genre defined by heavy, rounded strokes and a warmly exuberant character. The family is small, comprising just a Regular and an Oblique, but the focus is deliberate. Best suited to headlines, packaging and poster work where a bold retro personality is wanted.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Mamba as inspired by Cooper Black, designed by Gaspar Muñoz and available in two styles: Regular and Oblique.

name: Likan
classification_notes: Likan draws from Expressionist style and Gothic calligraphy with angular, broken strokes and sharp incisions, placing it firmly in the display category with blackletter heritage rather than a functional text face.
summary: An angular display face by Mauricio Adasme, drawing on Expressionist style and Gothic calligraphy, with sharp incisions evoking the volcanic terrain of the southern Chilean Andes.
description: Named after a Mapuzungun word relating to rock or flint, Likan uses broken, angular strokes and sharp cut entries that give the letterforms a raw, geological quality. The design bridges Expressionist graphic traditions and Gothic calligraphy, resulting in a face suited to editorial headlines, posters and identity work with a strong sense of place. Available in three weights, Regular through Black.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Likan as a typeface of broken strokes and incisive entries, built through references to Expressionist style and Gothic calligraphy. Its name comes from a word in Mapuzungun relating to rocks, sometimes translated as flint, and its volcanic appearance reflects the rugged landscapes of the southern Chilean Andes. Designed by Mauricio Adasme.

name: Konstanz
classification_notes: Described as grotesque in aesthetic while drawing on Bauhaus and Futura geometric influences, placing it within a geometric-inflected grotesque tradition rather than a pure geometric sans.
summary: A grotesque sans with Bauhaus and Futura references across eight weights, aimed at editorial and cultural-institution work.
description: Designed by Bruno Jara Ahumada, Konstanz draws on grotesque convention while reintroducing geometric gestures from Bauhaus and Futura, giving it a cooler, more institutional character than a straight revival. Sixteen styles span Thin through Black with matching italics, and a stylistic set extends its versatility. The family is pitched at museums, galleries, design biennials and similar cultural contexts, as well as editorial, poster and branding applications.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Konstanz as a sans serif family inspired by books and magazines for museums, art galleries, design biennials, architecture and theatre. They note its grotesque aesthetic foundation combined with geometric gestures drawn from Bauhaus and Futura, and position it for editorial projects, posters, branding and advertising across 16 styles including a stylistic set.

name: Munchies
classification_notes: Munchies employs reverse contrast stroke construction across a slab-serif structure, drawing on 19th-century wood type and Italian Caslon traditions, which places it firmly in the display slab category with an inverted stress axis.
summary: A reverse-contrast slab-serif in two subfamilies, Normal and Display, drawing on 19th-century wood letterpress and Italian Caslon traditions across six weights.
description: Designed by Gaspar Muñoz, Munchies inverts the conventional stress axis of a slab-serif, producing a striking optical effect suited to headlines and bold branding. The family splits into Normal, which carries a measured contrast reminiscent of Western poster lettering, and Display, which pushes that contrast to an extreme. Both subfamilies are available as variable fonts, and the character set includes small caps, ligatures and alternates for A, M, N, V, W, ampersand and arrows.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describes Munchies as a reverse-contrast slab-serif inspired by the scale of 19th-century wood letterpress blocks and the Italian Caslon language. The family spans 13 styles across two subfamilies, Normal and Display, each also offered as a variable font, with OpenType features including uppercase, lowercase, small caps, ligatures and alternate characters. They position it as ideal for headlines, bold branding and kinetic typography.

name: Moncler
classification_notes: Moncler is a high-contrast serif with dramatic stroke variation and renaissance-influenced forms, designed explicitly for titles and graphic display work rather than text setting.
summary: A high-contrast variable serif inspired by Dracula and renaissance art, built for dramatic titling across 21 styles and three width variants.
description: Designed by David Súid, Moncler draws from Bram Stoker's Dracula and the visual language of the renaissance to produce a typeface with pronounced stroke contrast and a theatrical character. The family spans seven weights from Thin to Black across Condensed, standard and Expanded subfamilies, totalling 21 styles, and is packaged as a variable font covering more than 220 weight and width combinations. Alternative symbols, a distinctive punctuation set and small-caps used as lowercase give it particular suitability for poster and graphic design work.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Moncler as rooted in the universe of horror fiction, specifically the character of Dracula from the classic novel, and as an exploration of renaissance art movement shapes and personality. The result is a variable font offering big contrast across 220-plus weights and widths, with alternative symbols, dramatic punctuation and small-caps as lowercase for titles and graphic design.

name: Mercurio Miente
designer: Patricio Truenos, David Súid, Alfredo Da Venezia
classification_notes: Designed explicitly as a political protest typeface referencing Chilean media activism, placing it firmly in the display category with a strong expressive and vernacular character rather than any conventional text classification.
summary: A single-style display typeface by three Chilean designers, conceived as a political tool referencing the 1967 anti-El Mercurio protest slogan.
description: Designed by Patricio Truenos, David Súid and Alfredo Da Venezia, this typeface takes its name and purpose from a Chilean protest phrase first used in 1967 to challenge right-wing media. Released as a free personal-use download, it functions as much as a piece of political commentary as a typographic artefact. The single style suits headline and poster work where expressive weight and historical resonance matter more than versatility.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Mercurio Miente as a free download font made for personal non-commercial use, conceived as a tool within the ongoing political and media landscape of Chile. The name references the banner Pedro Cardyn hung at the PUC campus in 1967, reading 'El Mercurio Miente' (The Mercurio Lies), a phrase the foundry say remains clear and relevant 54 years on.

name: Paprika
classification_notes: A handwritten casual script inspired by modern calligraphy trends, sitting between elegant and spontaneous depending on context.
summary: A casual handwritten script by Isabel La Rivera, balancing modern calligraphy elegance with a lightly messy, spontaneous character.
description: Designed by Isabel La Rivera as W Type Foundry's first script release, Paprika blends the polish of modern calligraphy with a loose, informal energy that shifts in register depending on how it is applied. The family includes capital swashes, contextual ligatures, alternate letterforms and an Extras set, giving designers room to vary the texture and rhythm of their compositions. Three styles are available: Regular, Expanded and Extras.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Paprika as a cute script with a slight messy touch, inspired by modern calligraphy trends. They note it can read as elegant or spontaneous depending on context, and highlight its capital swashes, contextual ligatures, alternate letters and extras as making it well suited to a range of graphic design applications. It marks the foundry's first script typeface, designed by new team member Isabel La Rivera.

name: Neo-Geo
classification_notes: Neo-Geo is a modular variable display typeface built from geometric shapes — squares, circles and stars — with no conventional letterform tradition, placing it firmly in the display category with a geometric constructivist subcategory.
summary: A modular variable display typeface by David Súid built from three geometric forms — circle, star and square — across nine fixed weights.
description: Developed through experiments with variable font technology, Neo-Geo applies a strict modular construction system to produce letterforms derived from the square, circle and star. The family ships in nine styles grouped across three shape variants, each available in regular, mid and black weights, with a variable version that interpolates between them. Intended for titles and short-copy contexts in contemporary graphic compositions.
foundry_blurb: Neo-Geo grew out of variable font technology experiments, proposing a modular construction system with defined rules. The typeface interpolates across modules and weights and takes its name from its contracting forms. Currently offering three shape variants — square, star and circle — the family is designed for titles and short text in contemporary compositions, with further variants planned.

name: Nutmeg
classification_notes: Described explicitly as geometric in structure, Nutmeg follows traditional geometric sans forms while adding distinctive terminal details that give it a warmer, slightly characterful quality beyond a strict neutral geometric.
summary: A geometric sans in 36 styles split across two subfamilies, one cleaner and rational, the other more expressive with flavoured terminal details.
description: Designed by Salvador Rodriguez and released in 2017, Nutmeg pairs a rational geometric structure with distinctive character details that push it beyond the neutral end of the genre. The family is divided into Nutmeg for clean text use and Nutmeg Headline for display work where its terminal flourishes become more pronounced. Each weight ships with OpenType alternates, ligatures, fractions, arrows and extended language support across nine weights with matching italics.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Nutmeg as a geometric typeface with a slight flavoured touch that remains comfortable at text sizes yet reveals display-worthy details at larger settings. The 36 fonts represent the first part of a larger planned family, split between a cleaner rational subfamily and a more characterful Headline version, both designed with powerful OpenType features including alternates, ligatures, fractions and extended language support.

name: Puffy
classification_notes: An ultra-heavy, inflated display typeface with pillowy, rounded forms so fat that counterforms collapse to a single line, placing it firmly in the novelty display category.
summary: A single-weight ultra-fat display typeface with inflated, cloud-like forms whose counterforms have been reduced to mere lines.
description: Designed by Gaspar Muñoz for W Type Foundry, Puffy pushes roundness to an extreme: strokes are so swollen that enclosed counters nearly disappear. A single Regular weight comes with stylistic alternates that shift the mood of the letterforms, offering a contrast between joyful and melancholic characters. Built for headlines and attention-grabbing applications where softness and visual impact are both required.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Puffy as an exploration into pillowy roundness, friendly and soft, looking almost like a cloud. So fat that its counterforms have been reduced to just one line, it is positioned as the perfect choice for conveying sympathy and is designed for headlines that don't want to go unnoticed. Stylistic options transform its counterforms to give it a double personality, shifting from joy to melancholy.

name: Rokha
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a wedge-serif, with sharp, exaggerated serifs inspired by Goudy's Copperplate Gothic, giving it a lapidary, carved-stone character suited to display use.
summary: A wedge-serif display face with sharply exaggerated serifs drawn from Copperplate Gothic, designed to read like carved stone across five weights.
description: Designed by Patricio Truenos Gaspar Muñoz, Rokha takes Goudy's Copperplate Gothic as its starting point and pushes the wedge serifs into something more aggressive and sculptural. Ten styles span five weights from Light to Dark, each paired with an oblique, and the inclusion of both upper and lowercase broadens its range beyond strictly titling use. Best deployed in headlines, posters and large-scale branding where its pointed serifs and commanding weight can do their work.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Rokha as a wedge-serif typeface with five weights plus obliques, inspired by Goudy's classic Copperplate Gothic. They characterise its serifs as exaggerated, pointy and strong, with a feel of carved stone that demands attention. The presence of upper and lowercase letters adds versatility, making it suited to display, headlines, posters and big branding.

name: Polyguard
classification_notes: Described explicitly as display-driven, with slightly wide proportions, vertical stroke endings and expanded terminals designed for high-impact headline and branding use rather than sustained text setting.
summary: A wide, industrial-flavoured display sans in six weights with matching obliques, built for headline and branding use.
description: Designed by Carlos Ávila, Polyguard pairs slightly expanded proportions with vertical stroke endings and widened terminals to create a sans that reads as structured but not mechanical. Six weights from Thin to Black each carry a matching oblique, giving twelve styles in total. Suited to packaging, signage and editorial work where presence at large sizes matters more than refinement at small ones.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Polyguard as a display-driven sans serif built around power, movement and agility, with slightly wide proportions for immediate presence and stability. They note that vertical stroke endings and expanded terminals introduce tension and distinctiveness, and position the family across branding, packaging, signage, editorial and commercial graphics requiring both strength and character.

name: Ryman Gothic
classification_notes: Described explicitly as combining American wood type gothic proportions with the contrast of wood types, placing it firmly in the American gothic sans tradition rather than European grotesque lineage.
summary: A hybrid gothic sans drawn from American wood type and Morris Fuller Benton's gothic tradition, with widened vowels to aid legibility.
description: Inspired by Edwin Allen and Morris Fuller Benton's American gothic letterforms, Ryman Gothic pairs classic gothic proportions with the higher contrast characteristic of wood type printing. Vowels are deliberately drawn wider than their consonant counterparts, easing readability in longer settings while giving the family a slightly warmer rhythm. The 18-style family spans nine weights from Thin to Heavy, each with a matching italic, and includes extended Latin, Cyrillic and Greek support alongside ligatures and arrow glyphs.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Ryman Gothic as a hybrid drawing on American wood types and gothic typefaces, citing the work of Edwin Allen and Morris Fuller Benton as primary references. The family combines gothic proportions with wood type contrast, and widens vowels to improve legibility in long-text contexts. It ships in 8 weights with matching italics across 18 styles, with Latin, Cyrillic and Greek language support.

name: Rosso Rounded
classification_notes: A condensed geometric display face with Art Deco influences and rounded terminals, positioning it firmly in the display category rather than a conventional geometric sans.
summary: A condensed Art Deco geometric with rounded terminals, offered in two subfamilies across five weights with italics throughout.
description: Derived from the original Rosso typeface, the rounded variant softens the geometric condensed structure with rounded forms that lend it a more approachable character. The family spans UltraLight to Black with matching italics, and a second subfamily, Rosso Rounded Alt, provides alternate glyphs for greater typographic variety. Suited to logotypes, packaging and headline work where a retro geometric flavour is needed without hard-edged severity.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Rosso Rounded as a rounded take on the original Rosso, blending Art Deco style with rounded forms for a modern and friendly aesthetic. They position it as versatile for branding and packaging, noting that alternate glyphs in the Alt subfamily allow for extra creative flexibility across logos, headlines and advertising posters.

name: Rosso
classification_notes: A condensed geometric sans with Art Deco proportions drawn from Roslyn Gothic, the type used on Philip K. Dick book covers; the Alt subfamily's expanded round forms push it further into display territory.
summary: A condensed geometric sans in two subfamilies, drawing on the Roslyn Gothic structure used for Philip K. Dick paperback covers and the decorative rhythms of Art Deco.
description: Rosso takes Roslyn Gothic as its structural reference, the condensed geometric letterform associated with 1970s science-fiction paperback covers, and extends it into a 10-weight family with matching italics. The Alt subfamily widens the round counters to produce a tighter, more contrasted rhythm typical of Art Deco display work. Alternative glyphs and discretionary ligatures, informed by Herb Lubalin's approach, broaden its range for logo-building, posters and cinematic titling. Both subfamilies are available as variable fonts.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Rosso as a condensed geometric sans with a retro character, built on the Roslyn Gothic structure that appeared on Philip K. Dick book covers in the 1970s. The Normal subfamily stays true to classic geometric proportions, while the Alt expands its round forms to produce a striking Art Deco contrast. Alternative glyphs and Herb Lubalin-inspired ligatures make it suited to logos, headlines, movie posters and volumetric work.

name: Salvatore
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a neo-grotesque, the family splits into a neutral cut and a more characterful Roman cut with distinctive terminals, placing it firmly in the grotesque sans tradition.
summary: A neo-grotesque in two flavours across 36 styles, split between a neutral cut and a more characterful Roman variant with distinctive terminals.
description: Described as the younger sibling of W Type Foundry's Nutmeg family, Salvatore divides its 36 styles evenly between Salvatore and Salvatore Roman. The former leans toward neutral, workhorse grotesque forms, while the Roman variant introduces more personality at character endings. The name nods to the Mad Men character Salvatore Romano, evoking the mid-1960s advertising context in which grotesque types dominated print communication.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Salvatore as the neo-grotesque younger brother of their Nutmeg family, offering 36 weights split into two flavours: Salvatore, with more neutral features, and Salvatore Roman, with greater versatility at the ends of characters. The name is drawn from the Mad Men character Salvatore Romano, a publisher working in the mid-1960s when grotesque typefaces ruled advertising.

name: Saes Grotesk
classification_notes: Directly inspired by nineteenth-century grotesque sans serifs, the design draws on historical Gothic and Grotesk specimens while incorporating contemporary letterform details, placing it firmly within the grotesque subcategory.
summary: A grotesque sans by Diego Aravena that borrows the imperfections of nineteenth-century type specimens and folds them into a contemporary 18-style family with variable font support.
description: Drawing on Gothic, Grotesque and Grotesk traditions from the 1800s, Saes Grotesk uses historical ink trapping errors and slightly extended letter terminals on forms like 'a', 'c' and 'e' to give the design an organic, handmade quality. A squared double-storey 'g' activates an alternate ligature texture in running text, adding a distinctly odd rhythm. The family spans nine weights from Thin to Heavy, each with a matching italic, and is also available as variable roman and italic fonts.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Saes Grotesk as a family inspired by the first sans serif typefaces of the nineteenth century, known variously as Gothics, Grotesques or Grotesks. The design combines the imperfections found in old grotesk specimens, such as a slightly uneven pound sign bar, with contemporary aesthetic choices, including extended letter endings and a squared 'g' that interacts with ligature features to create an unusual texture in text.

name: Sonny Gothic
classification_notes: Described explicitly as rational-geometric, inspired by the geometric style of the 1970s and Herb Lubalin's work; pure geometric construction with modern cap proportions and traditional 70s cap ligatures.
summary: A geometric sans in 36 styles across two subfamilies, drawing on the 1970s rational geometry and cap ligatures associated with Herb Lubalin.
description: Designed by Salvador Rodriguez at W Type Foundry, Sonny Gothic pairs modern cap proportions with the traditional cap ligatures characteristic of 1970s geometric lettering. The family runs from Thin to Ultrablack across upright and italic cuts, with a full Sonny Condensed subfamily extending the range to tighter settings. Each style includes alternate characters, ligatures, fractions, arrows and extended language support via OpenType features.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Sonny Gothic as their most rational-geometric typefamily to date, inspired by the geometric style of the 1970s and specifically Herb Lubalin's logos, typefaces and magazines. Beginning as a pure geometric design with modern cap proportions, it evolved when the designers combined those proportions with 70s traditional cap ligatures, resulting in a 36-style family across two subfamilies suited to a broad range of graphic design applications.

name: STGO TYPO
classification_notes: A collection of typefaces sourced from found lettering in Santiago's streets, spanning multiple styles from block to script-adjacent forms; firmly display in character and purpose.
summary: A ten-style family drawn from found lettering across Santiago's streets, gathered during 2023 field research by WTF Studio.
description: Each of the ten styles — from Marmol and Block through to Carreton and Tag — is derived from lettering observed across different districts of Santiago, preserving the visual diversity of the city's graphic vernacular. The project was developed with support from Chile's Fondo Nacional de las Artes y la Culturas, positioning it as documentary as much as typographic. Available for non-commercial use under a personal licence.
foundry_blurb: STGO TYPO is W Type Foundry's study and rescue project, collecting letterforms found across Santiago during 2023 explorations of the city. The ten resulting styles are made available for non-profit projects, with the work supported by Chile's Fondo Nacional de las Artes y la Culturas.

name: Sonny Gothic V.2
classification_notes: A geometric sans in the tradition of Avant Garde, with softened corners giving it a rounded, friendly character while retaining the ligature-driven rhythm of its predecessor.
summary: A rounded extension of Sonny Gothic with softened corners and an expanded ligature set drawn from the spirit of Herb Lubalin's Avant Garde.
description: Sonny Gothic V.2 reworks its predecessor by softening all corners to produce a warmer, more approachable texture across nine weights from Thin to Ultrablack, each with an italic. The ligature system is a central feature, building on Avant Garde's approach with new combinations designed specifically to manage the white space between rounded letterforms such as FL, FB and FR. OpenType features include alternate characters, small caps, fractions, arrows and extended language support, making it well suited to advertising and graphic design contexts.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Sonny Gothic V.2 as an extension of their popular Sonny Gothic, with all corners softened for a friendlier, fluffier visual language. The ligatures are inspired by Herb Lubalin's work on Avant Garde, with new pairs added to manage the whites created between softened characters. The family ships with 18 styles and a full suite of OpenType features including alternates, small caps, fractions and arrows.

name: Supera Gothic
classification_notes: Supera Gothic draws from both geometric (Erbar Grotesk) and humanist (Johnston) early 20th-century traditions, blending geometric structure with humanist proportions, placing it at the intersection of geometric and humanist sans-serif.
summary: A sans-serif family blending Erbar Grotesk's geometric precision with Johnston's humanist proportions across nine weights with matching italics and variable font support.
description: Drawing directly from Jakob Erbar and Edward Johnston, this family carries pointed crossbars on the 'f' and 't' in heavier weights as a nod to Erbar, while uppercase proportions and numerals echo Johnston's calligraphic sensibility. The 18-style family spans Thin to Ultrablack with matching italics, and is also available as roman and italic variable fonts. OpenType features are included, making it practical across editorial, branding and screen-based work.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Supera Gothic as a design inspired by early geometric and humanist typefaces of the 20th century, specifically Erbar Grotesk by Jakob Erbar and Johnston by Edward Johnston. Heavier weights feature pointed 'f' and 't' bars honouring Erbar, while uppercase letters and numerals reflect Johnston's proportions. The family comes in nine weights plus matching italics and includes a broad range of OpenType features, designed by Diego Aravena Salvador Rodriguez.

name: Ulises
classification_notes: Described explicitly as an eclectic slab serif with grotesque features, drawing on a modern serif tradition. The mix of slab structure with grotesque influences places it in an eclectic or hybrid slab category.
summary: A nine-weight slab serif with grotesque touches, intended for both headline and text setting across print and screen.
description: Designed by Salvador Rodriguez and Diego Aravena, Ulises spans nine weights from Thin to Heavy with matching italics, giving 18 styles in total. The family mixes slab serif structure with grotesque characteristics, a combination the foundry says widens its range of use across graphic design, publishing, web and motion work. Typefaces such as Shift, Brioni, Skolar and Malaga are cited as inspirations, situating Ulises within a contemporary editorial serif tradition.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Ulises as a modern serif type family available in nine weights with matching italics, inspired by typefaces including Shift, Brioni, Skolar and Malaga. Their goal was an eclectic slab serif with grotesque features to widen its usability, making it suitable for headlines and small text alike, and appropriate for graphic design, printed publications, web design, motion graphics and branding.

name: Urbani
classification_notes: Described as a blend of Neohumanist and Neogrotesque influences, inspired by Frutiger and Renner, placing it at the intersection of humanist warmth and grotesque rationality.
summary: A sans-serif blending Neohumanist and Neogrotesque influences across nine weights, designed with reading comfort and alternate glyph flexibility in mind.
description: Designed by Salvador Rodriguez and inspired by Adrian Frutiger and Paul Renner, Urbani sits between humanist warmth and grotesque rationality. Its subtly narrow proportions, tall ascenders, generous counter-forms and ink traps are all oriented toward extended text setting in print and digital contexts. The family ships with 18 styles including full italic coverage, plus a broad OpenType feature set encompassing alternate glyphs, old-style figures, smallcaps and ligatures.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Urbani as a fusion of Neohumanist and Neogrotesque traditions, with design choices including ink traps, wider counter-forms and slightly elevated ascenders all serving legibility. Alternative Glyphs via OpenType are positioned as central to achieving a universal, geometrical visual language across the family.

name: Throwup
classification_notes: Throwup is explicitly a display typeface drawn from graffiti 'flop' or 'bomb' lettering, characterised by thick-to-thin spray-paint transitions and simple, rapid forms — placing it firmly in the display category with a graffiti subcategory.
summary: A layered, colour-font display typeface drawn from graffiti 'flop' lettering, replicating the thick-to-thin strokes of spray paint caps.
description: Designed by Gaspar Muñoz from his own graffiti background, Throwup translates the rapid, simple forms of spray-painted 'bombs' into a digital layered type system. The family splits into two subfamilies across eight styles: a base set with Stroke, Shadow and Highlight layers, and a Colour variant in Yellow, Pink, Light Blue and Grey. The layering system allows for flexible combinations that reproduce the look of oversprayed graffiti lettering.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Throwup as a display typeface inspired by graffiti culture, specifically the 'flop' or 'bomb' style executed with spray paint and fatcap valves. Designer Gaspar Muñoz channels his graffiti background through Glyphs to produce a playful layered and colour font available in solid colour and multi-layer formats. It is free for personal, non-commercial use.

name: Variante Piñera
classification_notes: A conceptual display typeface where every letterform is marked with the rejection stamp used on Chilean pandemic aid applications, making it a political artefact rather than a conventional text face.
summary: A conceptual typeface by Diego Aravena in which every character bears the rejection mark used on Chilean state aid applications during the pandemic.
description: Designed as a political statement by Diego Aravena, the typeface draws on the bureaucratic symbol of insufficiency stamped on welfare applications under President Piñera's government. Every character the user types is rendered as rejected, turning text itself into a critique of neoliberal state policy and the marginalisation of Chile's working population. Available as a free personal-use download.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describes Variante Piñera as an exercise in political design, built around the rejection symbol applied to state aid claims during Chile's pandemic. They frame it as a free font through which everything you write will be rejected, offered as a reminder of how the Piñera government regarded its citizens.

name: Wordmark
classification_notes: The foundry describes a refreshed humanist style with attention to readability across text and display uses, consistent with the humanist sans tradition favoured in branding and signage.
summary: A 48-style humanist sans by Gaspar Muñoz built for branding, split into Normal and Display subfamilies each with condensed and italic cuts.
description: Designed by Gaspar Muñoz, this family draws on a humanist sans tradition suited to brand identities and signage. It splits into two complementary systems: Wordmark Normal, tuned for text readability across sizes, and Wordmark Display, drawn with a taller x-height for headlines and large-format applications. Six weights from Light to Heavy run across both subfamilies in full, regular and condensed widths, all with matching italics.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe Wordmark as a fully equipped branding tool with a refreshed humanist style, drawn by Gaspar Muñoz and intended as a successor to their earlier release Herokid. The family spans 48 styles across two subfamilies, Wordmark Normal for text and signage and Wordmark Display for headlines and screens, with condensed, expanded and italic options throughout.

name: WTF Forma
classification_notes: Described as inspired by DIN's clarity and structure, WTF Forma is a functional, orderly grotesque sans with a contemporary corporate character, spanning multiple widths from Compressed to Wide.
summary: A DIN-influenced grotesque spanning five widths and five weights, built for corporate brand systems that need warmth alongside formality.
description: Fifty styles cover five widths from Compressed to Wide, each in five weights from Thin to Heavy with matching italics, giving brand designers a single family that can handle everything from tight display headlines to running text. The design takes cues from DIN's regulated geometry while softening the result enough to feel approachable rather than purely technical. Aimed squarely at multinational companies and B2B contexts where legibility and a professional tone must coexist.
foundry_blurb: W Type Foundry describe WTF Forma as built with three priorities: big corporations, friendliness, and being a true solution. Inspired by DIN's clarity and structure, it delivers a contemporary voice for global brands — orderly and functional for long texts, short copy, titles and presentations, with 50 styles spanning Compressed, Condensed, Regular, Expanded and Wide widths.
